answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation*, Volume IV, Chapter 11 ("De l’influence de l’esclavage sur les mœurs de quelques peuples de l’Amérique méridionale, originaires d’Espagne" / "On the influence of slavery on the morals of some peoples of South America, of Spanish origin"), paragraph [p-cc46a4](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/11-de-l-influence-de-l-esclavage-sur-les-moeurs-de-qu/translation/#p-cc46a4).**[^1]

The chapter enumerates the five kinds of papal indulgence-bulls as follows: "The papal government delivered to the Spanish government, and the latter delivered to commerce in its colonies, **five kinds of bulls: the bull for the living, that for the dead, that for dairy and eggs, that of composition, and that of the crusade**.[^1] It then explains the "bull of composition" precisely as described: "The bull of composition had the marvelous effect of making the unjust holder of another's property the legitimate owner; the thief who, in the midst of a crowd of people, had managed to filch a well-filled purse, had only to go find the merchant of indulgences and deliver to him a small share of his prize, and both became legitimate possessors of the stolen property."[^1]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, [p-cc46a4](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/11-de-l-influence-de-l-esclavage-sur-les-moeurs-de-qu/translation/#p-cc46a4).

args.query: bull for the living, that for the dead, that for dairy and eggs
